genesi-1-s2-hist-000.claim_text: The "firmament" or "vault" (raqîaʿ, v.6) has been misread as a solid dome based on later Jewish cosmography, but the word means an expanse or spread-out space — the atmosphere. The Akkadian cognate (raqu) can mean to beat or spread thin, as a metalworker spreads metal, but the biblical usage consistently refers simply to the sky/atmosphere (cf. Ps 19:1; Ezek 1:22–26). The separation of waters above and below the firmament describes the hydrological cycle: atmospheric moisture above, seas below.
genesi-1-s2-hist-000.notes: The claim about raqîaʿ being misunderstood is disputed. Ancient Near Eastern cosmology, including biblical texts, often conceived of a solid dome-like structure, as evidenced in Job 37:18 and other passages.
genesi-1-s2-hist-000.fix_suggestion: The raqîaʿ in Genesis 1:6 likely refers to a solid dome-like structure consistent with ancient Near Eastern cosmology, though some passages use it more generally for sky/expanse.

genesi-1-ch-hebtext-009.claim_text: The piel participle of rāḥap — used elsewhere only of an eagle hovering over its young (Deut 32:11). The Spirit's presence over the waters is protective, maternal, preparatory. Creation is a Trinitarian act.
genesi-1-ch-hebtext-009.notes: The verb rachap appears elsewhere in the OT beyond Deuteronomy 32:11, including Jeremiah 23:9. The Trinitarian interpretation is theological, not linguistic.
genesi-1-ch-hebtext-009.fix_suggestion: The verb rachap occurs in other biblical texts besides Deuteronomy 32:11. The hovering/brooding imagery is present, but Trinitarian interpretation is theological rather than inherent in the Hebrew text.

exodus-10-ch-hebtext-005.claim_text: V.21: "Darkness that can be felt" (ḥōshek ʾĕphelyāh — lit. "darkness of darkness"). The plague simultaneously attacks Ra, the Egyptian sun-god, and foreshadows the Gospel's contrast: light has come into the world (John 1:5) and the darkness has not overcome it.
exodus-10-ch-hebtext-005.notes: The Hebrew phrase is ḥōshek wĕyāmēsh ḥōshek, not ḥōshek ʾĕphelyāh. The word ʾĕphelyāh appears in different contexts but not in Exodus 10:21.
exodus-10-ch-hebtext-005.fix_suggestion: The correct Hebrew is ḥōshek wĕyāmēsh ḥōshek (darkness that may be felt), with wĕyāmēsh being the key term for 'felt/touched.'

exodus-14-ch-tx-001.fix_suggestion: The Hebrew MT has 'east wind' while the LXX has 'south wind' - this represents a textual variant, not a translation difference of the same underlying text.

exodus-31-s1-hist-000.claim_text: Bezalel ('in the shadow of God') and Oholiab ('the Father's tent') are divinely appointed master craftsmen. The description of Bezalel being 'filled with the Spirit of God' for artistic skill is the first occurrence of Spirit-filling in the Bible. The range of skills listed — metalwork, gem-cutting, woodcarving, textile work — corresponds to the craft specialisations attested in Egyptian workshop scenes (tomb paintings at Thebes show metalworkers, jewellers, carpenters, and weavers working side
exodus-31-s1-hist-000.notes: Bezalel is not the first occurrence of Spirit-filling in the Bible; Joseph (Gen 41:38) and others precede him.
exodus-31-s1-hist-000.fix_suggestion: Bezalel represents an early and significant example of Spirit-filling for artistic skill, though not the first Spirit-filling mentioned in Scripture.

exodus-40-s1-hist-001.claim_text: The command to erect the Tabernacle on the first of the first month creates a precise one-year symmetry: Israel left Egypt on the fifteenth of Nisan; the Tabernacle stands on the first of Nisan, twelve months later. The year of wilderness formation — plagues, Passover, Red Sea, Sinai, the calf, the covenant renewal — culminates in God moving in. The entire year's journey was preparation for this moment.
exodus-40-s1-hist-001.notes: Israel left Egypt on the 15th of Nisan, but the tabernacle was erected on the 1st of Nisan of the second year, making it approximately 10.5 months, not exactly 12 months.
exodus-40-s1-hist-001.fix_suggestion: The time span was approximately 10.5 months, not exactly one year - from the 15th of Nisan (Exodus) to the 1st of Nisan of the second year (tabernacle erection).

exodus-9-ch-thread-005.fix_suggestion: Paul cites Exodus 9:16 in Romans 9:17 to illustrate God's sovereignty in raising up Pharaoh, but this is not specifically about the hail plague nor is Romans 9:17 the primary verse Paul builds election doctrine on.

number-2-ch-hebtext-005.claim_text: The march order mirrors the camp order — Judah sets out first, then Reuben, then the tabernacle carried by the Levites in the middle, then Ephraim, then Dan as the rear guard. number-2-ch-hebtext-005.notes: The tabernacle is carried by Levites in the middle, but the ark and most holy items were carried by Kohathites after other Levitical groups, not necessarily in the geographic center of the march.

The Kohathites with the holy objects followed after the first two divisions, creating a protected position but not necessarily the exact center.

number-33-s1-heb-001.notes: The Hebrew word massa' primarily means 'burden' or 'oracle,' not 'journey-stage.' The itinerary uses massa'ot for the actual journey stages.
number-33-s1-heb-001.fix_suggestion: The Hebrew word massa'ot (journey stages) appears in the itinerary, referring to the stages of Israel's wilderness journey.

ruth-2-ch-hebtext-017.claim_text: Boaz's instruction that Ruth "stay close" to his workers uses the verb šāmar (v.21-22), which carries the sense of careful watching and protective keeping. The same verb describes God keeping Israel (Num 6:24; Ps 121:3-8). Ruth gleaning "close to the women of Boaz's household" while under his protective oversight is a picture of the covenant community's protection of the vulnerable — enacted here by one man of integrity.
ruth-2-ch-hebtext-017.notes: The Hebrew verb in Ruth 2:21-22 for 'stay close' is דבק (dabaq), not שמר (shamar). Shamar does mean 'keep/watch/guard' but is not the verb used in this context.
ruth-2-ch-hebtext-017.fix_suggestion: Boaz's instruction that Ruth 'stay close' to his workers uses the verb dabaq (דבק), meaning 'to cling' or 'stick close to.' While shamar does carry protective connotations elsewhere in Scripture, the actual verb here emphasizes proximity and attachment rather than protective watching.

1kings-2-s2-heb-003.claim_text: Solomon tells Shimei to showḥesed(v.7 context, cf. 2 Sam 9) — the same covenant loyalty David showed to Mephibosheth. The term binds the political narrative to the theological: Solomon's consolidation is framed as covenant faithfulness, not mere power politics.
1kings-2-s2-heb-003.notes: Solomon tells the sons of Barzillai to show chesed in v.7, not Shimei. Shimei receives restrictions, not a call for chesed.
1kings-2-s2-heb-003.fix_suggestion: Solomon tells the sons of Barzillai to show chesed (v.7), not Shimei. Shimei receives house arrest and conditions (vv.36-46).

1kings-2-ch-hebtext-007.claim_text: Solomon tells Shimei to showḥesed(v.7 context, cf. 2 Sam 9) — the same covenant loyalty David showed to Mephibosheth. The term binds the political narrative to the theological: Solomon's consolidation is framed as covenant faithfulness, not mere power politics.
1kings-2-ch-hebtext-007.notes: In 1 Kings 2:7, David tells Solomon to show chesed to Barzillai's sons, not Solomon telling Shimei. Shimei is commanded to stay in Jerusalem (v.36-38), not to show chesed.
1kings-2-ch-hebtext-007.fix_suggestion: David tells Solomon to show chesed to the sons of Barzillai (v.7), referencing covenant loyalty. The Shimei narrative (vv.36-46) involves confinement to Jerusalem, not chesed.

1kings-4-ch-hebtext-000.notes: The Hebrew term in 1 Kings 4 is נִצָּבִים (niṣṣābîm), but this typically means 'stationed ones' or 'prefects,' not specifically 'officers/governors.'

2kings-1-s2-heb-000.notes: ʾish means 'man/person', not 'fire'. Fire is ʾēš (aleph-shin) with different vowels.

2kings-1-s2-heb-001.claim_text: Fire (ʾish) descends from heaven twice in this chapter (vv.10, 12), echoing the fire at Carmel (1 Kgs 18:38). Fire is YHWH’s characteristic manifestation — consuming, purifying, unanswerable.
2kings-1-s2-heb-001.notes: Based on incorrect Hebrew word. Fire is ʾēš, not ʾish. The theological point about fire as YHWH's manifestation is valid.
2kings-1-s2-heb-001.fix_suggestion: Fire (ʾēš) descends from heaven twice in this chapter

2kings-1-ch-hebtext-004.notes: Incorrect Hebrew word. 'Fire' is אֵשׁ (esh), not אִישׁ (ish) which means 'man.'

2kings-2-s2-heb-000.notes: The Hebrew term for 'youths' in 2 Kings 2:23 is נְעָרִים קְטַנִּים (ne'arim qetannim), not qĕlallîm.

2kings-21-ch-ppl-000.claim_text: [Hezekiah — Key biblical figure] Manasseh reigns 55 years — the longest reign of any king — and undoes everything Hezekiah built…
2kings-21-ch-ppl-000.notes: Manasseh's 55-year reign was long but not the longest of any king. Several kings had longer reigns, including Solomon (40 years) and David (40 years), and potentially others depending on interpretation.
2kings-21-ch-ppl-000.fix_suggestion: Manasseh reigns 55 years — one of the longest reigns of Judahite kings — and undoes everything Hezekiah built

2kings-21-ch-ppl-001.claim_text: [Manasseh — Key biblical figure] Manasseh reigns 55 years — the longest reign of any king — and undoes everything Hezekiah built…
2kings-21-ch-ppl-001.notes: Same issue as claim 1 - Manasseh's reign was not the longest of any king in biblical history.
2kings-21-ch-ppl-001.fix_suggestion: Manasseh reigns 55 years — one of the longest reigns of Judahite kings — and undoes everything Hezekiah built

2kings-22-ch-ppl-000.claim_text: [Joash — Key biblical figure] Josiah, now 26, orders temple repairs (echoing Joash in chapter 12)…
2kings-22-ch-ppl-000.notes: Confuses Josiah with Joash. Joash ordered temple repairs in 2 Kings 12, but this passage is about Josiah.
2kings-22-ch-ppl-000.fix_suggestion: Josiah, now 26, orders temple repairs (echoing Joash's temple repairs in 2 Kings 12)

2kings-22-ch-thread-001.claim_text: [Josiah Finds the Book of the Law → Deut 31:24–26, Connection] Moses commanded that the Book of the Law be placed beside the ark as a witness. Josiah discovers it in the temple — exactly where Moses put it.
2kings-22-ch-thread-001.notes: Moses commanded the book be placed beside the ark, but there's no evidence it was continuously there or that Josiah found it in that exact location.
2kings-22-ch-thread-001.fix_suggestion: The connection between Moses' command and Josiah's discovery location is speculative. The text doesn't specify where exactly in the temple Hilkiah found the book.

2kings-23-ch-ppl-000.claim_text: [Manasseh — Key biblical figure] He reads the Book of the Law publicly, renews the covenant, then systematically destroys: the Baal and Asherah vessels in the temple, the idolatrous priests, the high places from Geba to Beersheba, the Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom (the c…
2kings-23-ch-ppl-000.notes: This description is attributed to Manasseh but describes Josiah's reforms. Manasseh was actually known for promoting idolatry, not destroying it.
2kings-23-ch-ppl-000.fix_suggestion: This description should be attributed to Josiah, not Manasseh. Manasseh was known for promoting idolatry and undoing Hezekiah's reforms.

2kings-3-s1-cross-001.claim_text: [1 Kgs 22:7] Jehoshaphat asks “Is there no longer a prophet of the LORD?” — the identical question he asks here. His faithfulness is consistent.
2kings-3-s1-cross-001.notes: 1 Kings 22:7 records Jehoshaphat asking about prophets, but 2 Kings 3:11 has different wording. The claim of 'identical question' is inaccurate.
2kings-3-s1-cross-001.fix_suggestion: The questions are similar in intent but not identical in wording. In 1 Kings 22:7, Jehoshaphat asks if there's another prophet to inquire of; in 2 Kings 3:11, he asks if there's a prophet of the LORD present.

2kings-5-s2-heb-000.claim_text: gōʾēl (goel) = no God in all the earth
2kings-5-s2-heb-000.notes: gōʾēl means 'redeemer/kinsman-redeemer', not 'no God in all the earth'. This appears to be a significant error.
2kings-5-s2-heb-000.fix_suggestion: gōʾēl means 'redeemer' or 'kinsman-redeemer'. The phrase 'no God in all the earth' would be different Hebrew entirely.

2kings-5-ch-thread-001.claim_text: [Naaman the Leper — Faith from an Unexpected Source → Luke 4:27, Connection] Jesus cites this episode: “There were many lepers in Israel in Elisha’s time, but only Naaman the Syrian was cleansed.” The Nazareth synagogue erupts — Jesus is saying God’s grace crosses ethnic boundaries.
2kings-5-ch-thread-001.notes: Jesus says 'none of them was cleansed except Naaman the Syrian' - he doesn't say 'only Naaman the Syrian was cleansed' which changes the emphasis.
2kings-5-ch-thread-001.fix_suggestion: Jesus cites this episode: 'There were many lepers in Israel in Elisha's time, but none of them was cleansed except Naaman the Syrian.' The Nazareth synagogue erupts — Jesus is saying God's grace crosses ethnic boundaries.

2kings-5-ch-hebtext-004.claim_text: gōʾēl (goel) = no God in all the earth
2kings-5-ch-hebtext-004.notes: This is completely incorrect. Gōʾēl means 'redeemer/kinsman-redeemer' in Hebrew, not 'no God in all the earth.'

2kings-9-s2-cross-001.claim_text: [1 Kgs 21:19] “In the place where dogs licked up Naboth’s blood, dogs will lick up your blood.” Fulfilled at Naboth’s vineyard.
2kings-9-s2-cross-001.notes: 1 Kings 21:19 prophesies dogs licking Ahab's blood, but this was fulfilled in Samaria (1 Kgs 22:38), not at Naboth's vineyard.
2kings-9-s2-cross-001.fix_suggestion: The prophecy about dogs licking blood was fulfilled when Ahab died and his chariot was washed in Samaria (1 Kings 22:38), not at Naboth's vineyard.

job-28-s1-heb-001.claim_text: "There is a mine (māqōm) for silver and a place where gold is refined" (v.1). The chapter opens with human technological achievement: mining, smelting, tunnelling. We can find silver, gold, iron, copper, sapphires — we can dig through rock and overturn mountains. Human ingenuity is extraordinary.
job-28-s1-heb-001.notes: Job 28:1 uses moṣā' (מוֹצָא) meaning 'source/origin', not māqōm. The translation and Hebrew identification are incorrect.
job-28-s1-heb-001.fix_suggestion: Job 28:1 uses moṣā' (מוֹצָא) meaning 'source' or 'origin', not māqōm. The verse reads: 'Surely there is a source (moṣā') for silver...'

job-30-s2-heb-000.claim_text: אַךְ תַנִּים (ʾakh tannîm) = brother of jackals
job-30-s2-heb-000.notes: The Hebrew phrase is אַח תַּנִּים (ʾaḥ tannîm), not אַךְ תַנִּים. The word is אַח (brother), not אַךְ (surely/only).
job-30-s2-heb-000.fix_suggestion: אַח תַּנִּים (ʾaḥ tannîm) = brother of jackals

job-30-ch-hebtext-002.claim_text: אַךְ תַנִּים (ʾakh tannîm) = brother of jackals
job-30-ch-hebtext-002.notes: The Hebrew phrase is אַח תַּנִּים (ʾaḥ tannîm), not אַךְ תַנִּים. The word אַח means 'brother', not אַךְ which means 'only/surely'.
job-30-ch-hebtext-002.fix_suggestion: אַח תַּנִּים (ʾaḥ tannîm) = brother of jackals

job-5-ch-ppl-002.claim_text: [Satan — Key biblical figure] Satan is a key figure in Eliphaz Continues: Blessed Is the One Whom God Corrects.
job-5-ch-ppl-002.notes: Satan does not appear in Job 5 and is not relevant to Eliphaz's discourse about divine correction.
job-5-ch-ppl-002.fix_suggestion: Remove Satan reference as it is not connected to Job chapter 5 or Eliphaz's speech about divine correction.

prover-1-s1-cross-001.claim_text: [1 Kgs 4:29–34] Solomon's wisdom was God-given and legendary — he composed 3,000 proverbs and 1,005 songs. Proverbs opens by anchoring its authority in Solomon's name and divinely granted insight.
prover-1-s1-cross-001.notes: 1 Kings 4:32 states Solomon spoke 3,000 proverbs and 1,005 songs, but the claim reverses these numbers.
prover-1-s1-cross-001.fix_suggestion: Solomon composed 3,000 proverbs and 1,005 songs (not 1,005 proverbs and 3,000 songs)

prover-11-s2-heb-002.notes: The Hebrew in Proverbs 11 uses נֶפֶשׁ בְּרָכָה (nepeš bĕrākâ) not נְדִיבָה (nĕdîbâ). Different word entirely.
prover-11-s2-heb-002.fix_suggestion: נֶפֶשׁ בְּרָכָה (nepeš bĕrākâ) = soul of blessing/generous soul

prover-12-ch-src-000.claim_text: [Eshet chayil] Wife of noble character (12:4 and 31:10) -- the literary inclusion bracketing Proverbs as a whole.
prover-12-ch-src-000.notes: While both passages use 'eshet chayil,' Proverbs 12:4 uses 'eshet chayil' but Proverbs 31:10 uses the same phrase. However, this is not a literary inclusion 'bracketing Proverbs as a whole' - that's an overstatement.
prover-12-ch-src-000.fix_suggestion: Both passages contain references to noble/virtuous women, but describing this as a literary inclusion bracketing all of Proverbs overstates the structural relationship.

prover-13-s1-cross-001.claim_text: [Prov 11:30] Fruit of the righteous is a tree of life -- 13:12 is its second occurrence, now attached to fulfilled longing.
prover-13-s1-cross-001.notes: Prov 13:12 mentions 'tree of life' but this is not its second occurrence after 11:30. The phrase appears multiple times in Proverbs (3:18, 11:30, 13:12, 15:4).
prover-13-s1-cross-001.fix_suggestion: The 'tree of life' motif appears in Prov 3:18, 11:30, 13:12, and 15:4, making 13:12 the third occurrence, not the second.

prover-15-ch-rec-001.claim_text: [15:33 and humility] Humility comes before honour -- the sequence cited by James (4:6) and Peter (1 Pet 5:5).
prover-15-ch-rec-001.notes: Proverbs 15:33 says 'fear of the Lord is instruction in wisdom, and humility comes before honor.' James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5 quote Proverbs 3:34 about God opposing the proud, not 15:33.
prover-15-ch-rec-001.fix_suggestion: James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5 quote Proverbs 3:34 ('God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble'), not 15:33

prover-16-s1-heb-003.claim_text: A just peles and scales belong to the LORD; all the weights in the bag are his doing (v. 11). Even the instruments of commerce are God's concern. The word peles means an exact balance — the standard itself. When marketplaces are honest, they reflect divine order; when corrupt, they assault it.
prover-16-s1-heb-003.notes: The Hebrew word in Proverbs 16:11 is פֶּלֶס (peles), not 'peles'. More importantly, פֶּלֶס means 'scales' or 'balance', not specifically 'exact balance' as claimed.
prover-16-s1-heb-003.fix_suggestion: פֶּלֶס (peles) means scales or balance, referring to the weighing instrument itself rather than specifically meaning 'exact balance' or 'the standard itself'

prover-21-ch-src-001.claim_text: [Hezekian editorial work] Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs.
prover-21-ch-src-001.notes: Proverbs 25:1 refers specifically to chapters 25-29, not chapter 21.
prover-21-ch-src-001.fix_suggestion: Proverbs 25:1 refers to the collection in chapters 25-29, not chapter 21.

prover-25-s2-cross-001.claim_text: [Rom 12:20] If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink. In doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head — Paul quotes v.21-22 directly.
prover-25-s2-cross-001.notes: Paul quotes Proverbs 25:21-22, not v.21-22. The verse reference is incorrect.
prover-25-s2-cross-001.fix_suggestion: Paul quotes Proverbs 25:21-22 directly in Romans 12:20

prover-26-s2-heb-000.notes: נִרְגָּן (nirgān) appears to be incorrect form/spelling for Hebrew word meaning gossip/whisperer.
prover-26-s2-heb-000.fix_suggestion: The correct Hebrew word for gossip/whisperer in Proverbs context is typically נִרְגָּן (nirgan) but this specific form needs verification, or may be רָכִיל (rakil) or לָחַשׁ (lachash)

prover-29-ch-tx-000.claim_text: [29:1–27: Chapter 29 — end of the Hezekian collection] Chapter 29 concludes the Hezekian collection (25–29). MT and LXX diverge on several individual proverbs, with the LXX adding material in some verses and condensing in others. The chapter's final verse ('Many seek an audience with a ruler') is rendered differently in LXX, which emphasizes divine rather than royal favor.
prover-29-ch-tx-000.notes: Chapter 29 concludes the second Solomonic collection (25-29), not specifically the 'Hezekian collection.' While these chapters are attributed to Hezekiah's men, the collection itself is Solomonic.

prover-3-s1-hist-000.claim_text: Proverbs 3:5–6 is perhaps the most quoted verse in the entire book. It functions as a practical summary of everything Proverbs teaches about the relationship between human wisdom and divine guidance. The word "lean" (šāʿan) describes physical weight — you lean on a wall, a staff, a person. To lean on your own understanding is to treat your own perception of reality as the load-bearing structure of your life. The correction is not to stop thinking but to hold your thinking within a posture of tru
prover-3-s1-hist-000.notes: The verb in Proverbs 3:5 is šāʿan (lean), not in verse 6 as context suggests. Also claim appears incomplete.
prover-3-s1-hist-000.fix_suggestion: The Hebrew verb šāʿan (lean) appears in verse 5, not describing verse 6. The verse teaches dependence on God rather than self-reliance.

prover-31-s2-heb-004.notes: הַלְלוּהָ means 'praise her' but this specific form doesn't appear in Prov 31:31. The text uses תְּהַלְּלוּהָ (tehallĕlûhā).
prover-31-s2-heb-004.fix_suggestion: תְּהַלְּלוּהָ (tehallĕlûhā) = 'let them praise her' or 'may they praise her'

prover-7-s1-hist-000.claim_text: The opening (vv.1–5) is the shortest instruction preamble in chs.1–9 — just five verses before the narrative begins. Its urgency is striking: "guard my teachings as the apple of your eye" (v.2). The "apple of your eye" (bābat ʿên, literally "daughter of the eye" — the pupil) is the most instinctively protected thing a person has. That is the level of vigilance required. Verse 4's instruction to call wisdom "sister" and insight "relative" is the positive alternative to the coming scene: if wisdom
prover-7-s1-hist-000.notes: Contains error: 'apple of eye' is ʾîšôn ʿayin, not 'bābat ʿên' (daughter of eye). Mixed up Hebrew idioms.
prover-7-s1-hist-000.fix_suggestion: The 'apple of your eye' is ʾîšôn ʿayin (literally 'little man of the eye'), not bābat ʿên ('daughter of the eye'). Both refer to the pupil but are different Hebrew expressions.

prover-9-ch-hebtext-002.claim_text: יִרְאַת יְהוָה רֵאשִׁית חָכְמָה (yirʾat yhwh rēʾšît ḥokmāh) = the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom

eccles-7-s2-cross-001.claim_text: [Rom 3:10–12] "There is no one righteous, not even one" — Paul’s quotation of this tradition.

eccles-7-s2-cross-001.fix_suggestion: Paul quotes Psalms 14 and 53 in Romans 3:10-12, not Ecclesiastes. While both texts affirm universal sinfulness, there's no direct quotation relationship.

songof-2-ch-thread-005.fix_suggestion: Matthew 24:32 uses fig tree imagery for recognizing the signs of the end times, which is thematically distinct from Song of Solomon's romantic botanical imagery.

jeremi-18-ch-hebtext-003.claim_text: The clay (ḥōmer) is "marred in the potter's hands" (v.4) — the Hebrew nišḥat suggests the clay was ruined or spoiled. Rather than discarding it, the potter reshapes it "as seemed best to him." The image implies that God's original design for Israel has been marred by sin, but God has not abandoned the project — he is reworking the material.
jeremi-18-ch-hebtext-003.notes: The Hebrew in Jer 18:4 is נשחת (nišḥat) from š-ḥ-t, not the root claimed. The interpretation of marring/spoiling is correct.
jeremi-18-ch-hebtext-003.fix_suggestion: The clay is 'marred in the potter's hands' (v.4) — the Hebrew nišḥat (from š-ḥ-t) indicates the clay was spoiled or ruined.

jeremi-3-ch-hebtext-003.claim_text: The noun mĕšubâ derives from the same root šûb but with a negative prefix — it means "turning away" rather than "turning back." Israel is called mĕšubâ (faithless/backsliding), a pun on the very verb of repentance. She has turned, but in the wrong direction.
jeremi-3-ch-hebtext-003.notes: מְשֻׁבָה does not have a 'negative prefix' - it's a Qal passive participle feminine form meaning 'turning away/backsliding'.
jeremi-3-ch-hebtext-003.fix_suggestion: מְשֻׁבָה (mĕšubâ) is a Qal passive participle meaning 'turning away/backsliding' - it derives from šûb but indicates the completed state of having turned away, not a prefixed negative form.

jeremi-3-ch-hebtext-004.claim_text: אַרְגָּעָה (ʾargāʿâ) = I will heal / I will not be angry forever
jeremi-3-ch-hebtext-004.notes: אַרְגָּעָה means 'I will not be angry/rage forever' - it's from רגע not רפא. The healing language comes from different verses.
jeremi-3-ch-hebtext-004.fix_suggestion: אַרְגָּעָה (ʾargāʿâ) means 'I will not be angry/rage forever' (from the root רגע). The healing language uses different vocabulary (רפא) in other verses.

jeremi-30-s2-heb-000.notes: תְּעָלָה (te'ala) is not found in Jeremiah 30:17. The text uses אֲרֻכָה (arukah) for healing/restoration.

jeremi-30-s2-heb-001.claim_text: "I will restore you to health (te'ala) and heal your wounds" (v.17). The word te'ala means the fresh growth of flesh over a wound. After diagnosing an incurable wound (cf. 15:18, the "deceptive brook"), God now promises to heal what he declared unhealable. The healer was always present; the patient was unwilling. Now both willingness and healing converge.
jeremi-30-s2-heb-001.notes: Jeremiah 30:17 uses אֲרֻכָה (arukah), not תְּעָלָה. The description of te'ala as 'fresh growth of flesh' may confuse it with other healing terms.
jeremi-30-s2-heb-001.fix_suggestion: The Hebrew uses אֲרֻכָה (arukah) meaning healing/restoration, not תְּעָלָה

jeremi-37-ch-hebtext-000.notes: נָשָׁא primarily means 'to lift up, carry, bear' not 'deceive/delude' which is typically נָשָׁה or other terms.

jeremi-37-ch-hebtext-001.claim_text: "Do not deceive yourselves (tashshi'u naphshotekem)" (v.9). The reflexive form emphasizes self-deception: "do not lift up your own souls" with false hope. When the Egyptian army approaches and the Babylonians temporarily withdraw, the people assume the siege is over. Jeremiah warns: the Babylonians will return and burn the city.
jeremi-37-ch-hebtext-001.notes: The verb is תַּשִּׁיאוּ from נָשָׁה (deceive), not from נָשָׂא. The etymology explanation conflates different roots.
jeremi-37-ch-hebtext-001.fix_suggestion: The verb תַּשִּׁיאוּ comes from נָשָׁה (to deceive), not נָשָׂא (to lift up). The warning is against self-deception about the temporary Babylonian withdrawal.

lament-2-ch-hebtext-000.notes: The Hebrew word is אֹיֵב (ʾōyēb), not יָעִיב (yāʿîb). The transcription and vocalization are incorrect.

lament-2-ch-hebtext-001.claim_text: "The Lord is like an enemy (āʿib); he has swallowed up Israel" (v.5). The most theologically shocking statement in the book. God himself has destroyed: his dwelling (v.2), his altar (v.7), his temple (v.7), his festival and Sabbath (v.6). The king and priest are rejected (v.6). The wall weeps (v.8). The gates sink into the ground (v.9). The elders sit on the ground in silence, dust on their heads; the young women bow down (v.10).
lament-2-ch-hebtext-001.notes: The Hebrew word should be אֹיֵב (ʾōyēb), not āʿib. The description of God's destruction is accurate to the text's content.
lament-2-ch-hebtext-001.fix_suggestion: The Lord is like an enemy (ʾōyēb); he has swallowed up Israel

lament-5-s1-hist-000.claim_text: The broken acrostic is the most debated structural feature in Lamentations. Chapters 1–4 follow the Hebrew alphabet; chapter 5 has 22 verses (the right count) but the letters are NOT in order. Scholars debate whether this is deliberate (form fracturing under grief) or simply a different compositional choice. The pastoral reading is compelling: when suffering exceeds language’s capacity to order it, even the alphabet fails. The poem is communal throughout: "our" appears in nearly every verse. Thi
lament-5-s1-hist-000.notes: Lamentations 5 is NOT a broken acrostic - it simply lacks acrostic structure entirely. Chapters 1, 2, 4 are acrostic; chapter 3 is triple acrostic; chapter 5 is not acrostic at all.
lament-5-s1-hist-000.fix_suggestion: Lamentations 5 has 22 verses but is not an acrostic poem at all, unlike chapters 1-4 which follow acrostic patterns.

ezekie-24-s1-hist-000.claim_text: On the exact day Nebuchadnezzar began the siege of Jerusalem (January 15, 588 BC), God gave Ezekiel this parable. The word also carries overtones of the 'ban' (kherem) in holy war contexts, suggesting that Pharaoh's capture is an act of sacred destruction.

ezekie-36-ch-hebtext-013.claim_text: The comparison to a flock evokes both Eden (paradise restored) and the festival flocks that filled Jerusalem at Passover, Weeks, and Tabernacles. The cities will be as full of people as Jerusalem was full of sacrificial animals during the great pilgrim feasts. The image combines agricultural abundance with cultic celebration.

It is divided into three horizontal strips: the northern strip (25,000 x 10,000) contains the sanctuary at its center and houses the Zadokite priests; the middle strip (25,000 x 10,000) is assigned to the Levites for their service; the southern strip (25,000 x 5,000) belongs to the city and is open to

ezekie-46-ch-thread-001.claim_text: [The Prince's Worship, Land Inheritance, and Temple Kitchens → Num 28:9-10, Connection] The Mosaic Sabbath offering was two male lambs. ezekie-46-ch-thread-001.notes: Numbers 28:9-10 prescribes two lambs for the Sabbath offering, but Ezekiel 46:4-5 prescribes six lambs and a ram for the Sabbath, not as an expansion but as the prince's personal offering alongside the regular offerings.
ezekie-46-ch-thread-001.fix_suggestion: Ezekiel 46:4-5 describes the prince's personal Sabbath offering (six lambs and a ram), which is separate from and additional to the regular Mosaic Sabbath offering of two lambs in Numbers 28:9-10.

ezekie-9-s1-hist-000.claim_text: God summons seven agents of judgment: one scribe in linen with a writing kit, and six executioners with destroying weapons. ezekie-9-s1-hist-000.fix_suggestion: God summons six agents of judgment: one scribe in linen with a writing kit, and five executioners with destroying weapons.

hosea-13-ch-thread-007.claim_text: [The Death of Ephraim → 1 Cor 15:55, Fulfilment] Paul quotes Hosea 13:14 as a victory cry over death: 'Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?'
hosea-13-ch-thread-007.notes: Paul quotes Hosea 13:14 in 1 Cor 15:55 but changes the wording significantly. The Hebrew is questions ('Where are your plagues/thorns?') while Paul makes it victory statements.
hosea-13-ch-thread-007.fix_suggestion: Paul alludes to Hosea 13:14 in 1 Cor 15:55 but transforms the Hebrew's rhetorical questions into declarations of victory over death.

haggai-1-s1-hist-000.claim_text: Haggai's opening oracle is the most precisely dated prophetic utterance in the Hebrew Bible: the first day of the sixth month (Elul) in the second year of Darius I, corresponding to August 29, 520 BC. This precision is not incidental but programmatic: Haggai's prophecy is embedded in concrete historical time, tethered to the Persian imperial calendar and the post-exilic community's daily reality. The historical context is critical: the returned exiles under Zerubbabel had laid the temple foundat
haggai-1-s1-hist-000.notes: August 29, 520 BC is incorrect. The first day of the sixth month in Darius's second year corresponds to August 29, 520 BC in some calendars, but this specific date calculation is disputed.
haggai-1-s1-hist-000.fix_suggestion: The date corresponds to approximately late August/early September 520 BC, but the exact date of August 29 is not definitively established due to calendar conversion complexities.

haggai-1-ch-ppl-002.claim_text: [Joshua son of Jozadak — Moses’s military commander and designated successor] Joshua son of Nun serves as Moses’s lieutenant throughout the wilderness period — commanding at Rephidim (Exod 17), ascending Sinai with Moses (Exod 24), guarding the tent of meeting. In Num 13–14 he stands with Caleb as one of two faithful spies. He is formally commissioned in Num 27 through Moses’s hand-laying and inherits the leadership role that Moses cannot fulfil past the Jordan.
haggai-1-ch-ppl-002.notes: This confuses two different Joshuas. Joshua son of Jozadak was a post-exilic high priest, not Moses's successor.
haggai-1-ch-ppl-002.fix_suggestion: Joshua son of Jozadak was the high priest who returned from Babylonian exile with Zerubbabel (Ezra 3:2, Haggai 1:1). He is not the same person as Joshua son of Nun, who was Moses's successor and conquered Canaan.

acts-1-ch-ppl-001.claim_text: [Peter↗ People — Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church] Peter (Simon bar Jonah) dominates Acts 1–12 as the church’s primary spokesman and miracle-worker. He preaches at Pentecost (Acts 2), heals at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3), defends before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4–5), raises Tabitha (Acts 9:36–42), and receives the vision that opens the gospel to Gentiles (Acts 10). ezra-4-ch-hebtext-007.claim_text: Artaxerxes orders the work to "cease" (bəṭēl) by force. The same verb describes the creation-rest of Gen 2:2 (God ceased). Here the cessation is forced, not voluntary — a perversion of Sabbath.
ezra-4-ch-hebtext-007.notes: The verb in Gen 2:2 is שָׁבַת (šāḇaṯ), not בְּטֵל (bəṭēl). These are different Hebrew roots with different meanings.
ezra-4-ch-hebtext-007.fix_suggestion: The cessation verb here is בְּטֵל (bəṭēl), which is different from שָׁבַת (šāḇaṯ) used for God's rest in Gen 2:2. The theological comparison between forced cessation and Sabbath rest is interpretive but based on different Hebrew words.
